summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the ubiquitous adoption of XML as the default format for configuration files, data storage, and structured text, arguing that its verbosity creates an 'angle bracket tax' that obscures actual information. He acknowledges XML's democratic appeal—it works and everyone uses it—but contends that because XML purports to represent everything, it ends up representing nothing particularly well. The post highlights alternatives like YAML and JSON that offer cleaner, more readable representations of the same data. Atwood doesn't declare XML fundamentally broken, but challenges developers to question whether it's truly the simplest solution for their specific use case rather than accepting it as the mindless default.
key_points: XML has become the default choice for nearly all text-based data storage, from config files to build scripts to comments, regardless of whether it's the best fit
The verbosity of XML buries actual information in 'an astounding amount of noise' with angle brackets and tags
XML was originally designed for document scenarios, not the data scenarios (SOAP, config files, databases) where it's now most commonly used
Alternatives like YAML and JSON offer cleaner, more human-readable representations with less syntactic overhead
The problem isn't XML itself, but its mindless, blanket application as a universal solution
money_quotes: If it's stored in a text file and needs to be retrieved and parsed, it's probably XML.
XML is like democracy. Sometimes it even works.
How much actual information is communicated here? Precious little, and it's buried in an astounding amount of noise.
Because XML purports to represent everything, it ends up representing nothing particularly well.
I spend a disproportionate amount of my time wading through an endless sea of angle brackets and verbose tags desperately searching for the vaguest hint of actual information.
It sure feels like, for any given representation of data in XML, there was a better, simpler choice out there somewhere.
Please think twice before subjecting yourself, your fellow programmers, and your users to the XML angle bracket tax.
key_insight: XML's biggest flaw isn't that it's technically bad, but that its 'represent anything' universality has led developers to use it mindlessly as the default when simpler, more appropriate alternatives exist.
